faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Common questions about glamping properties, feasibility studies, and outdoor hospitality

items.question: What is a glamping feasibility study?
items.answer: A glamping feasibility study evaluates whether a proposed glamping property will be financially viable and operationally successful. It includes market analysis, financial projections, site assessment, and competitive analysis to help investors make informed decisions about their outdoor hospitality investment.

items.question: How do I find glamping properties near me?
items.answer: Use our interactive map to search for glamping properties by location. We have 650+ verified properties across the United States, Canada, and Europe. You can filter by location, property type, amenities, and price range to find the perfect glamping destination for your outdoor adventure.

items.question: What's the difference between glamping and camping?
items.answer: Glamping (glamorous camping) combines the outdoor experience of camping with the comfort and amenities of a hotel. Unlike traditional camping, glamping accommodations typically include comfortable beds, electricity, heating, and often private bathrooms and kitchens. It offers nature immersion without sacrificing modern conveniences.

items.question: How much does a glamping feasibility study cost?
items.answer: Glamping feasibility studies typically cost between $8,000 and $15,000, depending on the scope, property size, and complexity. Factors include market research depth, financial modeling requirements, and site assessment needs. More comprehensive studies with detailed analysis may cost $20,000 or more.

items.question: What should I look for in a glamping property?
items.answer: Key factors include location and accessibility, unique accommodation types, quality amenities (wifi, bathrooms, kitchen facilities), safety features, and proximity to attractions. Our database includes detailed information on 650+ properties including ratings, amenities, and pricing to help you compare options.

items.question: Are glamping properties profitable?
items.answer: Profitability varies significantly based on location, occupancy rates, pricing strategy, and operational costs. A professional feasibility study can provide market-specific revenue projections and help determine if a property will be profitable in your target market. Many successful glamping properties achieve over 60% occupancy rates with strong seasonal demand.

rvIndustryOverview.title: RV Industry Overview

chartDetails.summaryAria: Expand chart methodology, source rules, and scan notes

rvIndustryOverview.subtitle: 2025 regional ARDR and occupancy from Campspot and RoverPass.
rvIndustryOverview.unitFilterHint: Charts use classified Campspot and RoverPass rows for the selected unit type. Comparison charts always show all three types; RV parking charts use RV Sites only. Unclassified rows are excluded (see below).
rvIndustryOverview.unitFilterUnclassified: Unclassified rows excluded (scan-wide): {total, number} — Campspot {campspot, number}, RoverPass {roverpass, number}. These rows never enter any chart fold.
rvIndustryOverview.unitFilterUnclassifiedNone: No unclassified rows in the last scan (every scanned row mapped to RV Sites, Tent Sites, or Lodging).
rvIndustryOverview.dataSourcesSummary: Aggregates are built from one unified scan of Campspot and RoverPass (open RoverPass listings only). Row counts by source appear in the cache bar, source transparency panels, and chart footers.
rvIndustryOverview.dataSourcesYoYNote: Year-over-year (2024 vs 2025) charts need both years’ annual occupancy and ARDR in standard bands on the same row. RoverPass listings usually have 2025-only rate/occupancy in our pipeline (no 2024 annual pair), so 2024 bars and state matched-cohort pop-ups are primarily Campspot; 2025 bars and 2025-only maps can include RoverPass when classified. Use the Campspot-only source toggle for YoY without RoverPass.
rvIndustryOverview.dataSourcesClassifiedRows: “Classified” means unit type was inferred from unit_type, description, and property name (same rules for Campspot and RoverPass text fields).
rvIndustryOverview.standardFiltersIntro: The same row-level rules run everywhere on this page for the selected unit type. A value is only averaged or counted when it falls inside the standard band for that metric. There is no percentile winsorizing beyond these fixed bounds.
rvIndustryOverview.standardFiltersOccupancy: Occupancy (any year used in a chart): after parsing (fractions 0–1 are scaled to percent), include only values from 10% through 99% inclusive. Values below 10% or at 100% are excluded as unreliable or invalid.
rvIndustryOverview.standardFiltersRate: Retail daily rate / ARDR (USD): include only values from $10 through $3,000 per night inclusive for whichever field that chart uses (2024 annual avg. retail daily rate, 2025 avg. retail daily rate, or seasonal weekday/weekend columns on season charts). Retail Daily Rate YTD is not used on this page.
rvIndustryOverview.standardFiltersPropertyCohort: Property- and RV-only charts (amenity penetration, RV parking mix, unit-type distribution): a row is included only when 2025 occupancy and 2025 ARDR from the average retail daily rate 2025 column both pass the standard bands, so denominators stay aligned with rate-based charts.
rvIndustryOverview.standardFiltersByChart: Regional map labels and state ADR choropleth use 2025 ARDR (average retail daily rate 2025) in band; the regional map also requires 2025 occupancy in band (Lodging uses a higher ARDR ceiling on the regional map only). State hover/pop-up YoY uses a matched cohort with 2024 and 2025 annual occupancy and ARDR each in band — primarily Campspot because RoverPass rows lack 2024 annual fields in this dataset. Trends, resort size, season/surface bars, amenities, and unit-type comparison charts apply the band to each metric as it enters that chart; 2024 columns on YoY charts are mostly Campspot, 2025 columns can include RoverPass.
rvIndustryOverview.mapCaption: 2025-only regional labels: unweighted means across classified Campspot and RoverPass rows with both 2025 ARDR (average retail daily rate 2025 column) and 2025 occupancy in band (regional ARDR band is $10–$3k for RV/Tent and $10–$10k for Lodging).
rvIndustryOverview.mapInteractHint: Hover or click a state for matched-cohort 2024 vs 2025 averages (YoY pop-up — primarily Campspot; see source note below the map). Regional fill colors use 2025 data only and can include RoverPass.
rvIndustryOverview.rowsScannedDetail: That count is every Campspot and RoverPass row read in this pass (open RoverPass listings only), up to each source’s row cap, even when a row does not qualify for a given chart.
rvIndustryOverview.mapOutliers: Fixed-band filtering (not percentile-based): occupancy must be between 10% and 99% (100% excluded); retail rates must be between $10 and $3,000 USD for the field that chart uses. See “Standard data filters” below for chart-by-chart detail.
rvIndustryOverview.mapRegionalInclusion: Regional 2025 map averages include classified Campspot and RoverPass rows whose state maps to one of the five regions, whose 2025 occupancy parses as a non-negative number (values from 0–1 are treated as fractions and scaled to percent) and lies between 10% and 99% (100% excluded), and whose 2025 ARDR from the average retail daily rate 2025 column lies between $10 and $3,000 for RV and Tent, or between $10 and $10,000 for Lodging (glamping). Retail Daily Rate YTD and seasonal columns are not used for these regional averages. Currency symbols and thousands separators in text fields are normalized before parsing. Rows are excluded if the state is missing or outside those regions, if the row does not classify to a unit type, or if occupancy or the 2025 rate is blank, “no data”, non-numeric, or outside the applicable bands.
rvIndustryOverview.mapStateCohortNote: Per-state hover and pop-up metrics use a matched YoY cohort: rows with valid occupancy and ARDR in both 2024 and 2025 (annual columns), each in standard bands — effectively Campspot-only because RoverPass rows in this pipeline do not populate 2024 annual fields. ARDR uses the average retail daily rate columns per year. Regional region colors and the state ADR choropleth use 2025 ARDR only and can include classified RoverPass rows.

trends.chartTitle: RV Resort / Park Occupancy Rate Trends
trends.intro: Occupancy and ARDR trends (2024–2025 only). 2024 series use full-year annual columns (primarily Campspot). 2025 series use average retail daily rate 2025 and 2025 occupancy and can include classified RoverPass rows. The U.S. column pools all regions. No 2023 data is included.
trends.methodology: Unweighted means across classified Campspot and RoverPass rows that have both metrics for each region/year bucket after standard filtering. 2024 buckets: primarily Campspot (annual 2024 columns). 2025 buckets: Campspot + RoverPass when classified. U.S. totals pool all qualifying rows in the five regions.

sizeImpact.chartTitle: Impact of Resort Size on ARDR & Occupancy
sizeImpact.intro: Avg. retail daily rates: pricing is firmer, and ARDR increased from full-year 2024 to full-year 2025 in each size band in this sample. The Northeast and largest parks tend to lead on rate; value-oriented bands remain comparatively lower. Overall, the pattern suggests stable occupancy with improving pricing power where 2025 data is available.
sizeImpact.body: ARDR (avg. retail daily rate): larger parks typically carry a rate premium versus smaller bands; the table below shows means for each size tier (2024 full year vs 2025 full year) from classified Campspot and RoverPass rows. Resort size uses property total sites when present, otherwise quantity of units. Large is 100+ sites, medium 50–99, small 25–49 (other counts are excluded).
sizeImpact.tableFootnote: ARDR change is percent change from 2024 to 2025. Occupancy and ARDR use annual columns per source (Campspot 2024/2025 fields; RoverPass where populated). Standard occupancy and ARDR band filtering applies per year. 2024 columns are mostly Campspot.

unitTypeByRate.subtitle: Avg. Retail Daily Rate Before Taxes and Booking Fees
unitTypeByRate.intro: Mean 2025 ARDR (avg. retail daily rate) by classified unit type across Campspot and RoverPass rows in the five U.S. regions. Rows need a parseable unit type (Glamping, RV Site, or Tent Site), state in-region, and a positive average retail daily rate 2025.
unitTypeByRate.methodology: Unweighted mean of row-level rates within each type. Rows must pass standard 2025 occupancy and 2025 ARDR (average retail daily rate 2025 column) bands. No taxes or booking-fee columns are added; values reflect the same retail rate fields used elsewhere on this page.
unitTypeByRate.noData: No rows matched the cohort for this chart.

unitTypeDistribution.subtitle: Share of sites by unit type, averaged across properties
unitTypeDistribution.intro: For each property (name, state, city), site counts use quantity of units when at least 1, otherwise 1 per row. Mix percentages are computed from classified Glamping / RV / Tent rows only, then averaged across properties with at least one classified site. Each property counts equally in that average: this is not the same as total glamping sites divided by total RV sites nationwide (a small bar here can still reflect many glamping sites if they sit on few properties).
unitTypeDistribution.methodology: Classification order matches the RV Industry Overview unit-type rules (same priority as glamping market unit tagging). Only rows in the five U.S. regions are included, with standard 2025 occupancy and 2025 ARDR (average retail daily rate 2025 column) band filtering applied before counting site mix. The three bar values are means of property-level mixes and always sum to about 100%; they answer “what is the typical property’s mix?” not “what share of all unit-rows are glamping?”
unitTypeDistribution.noData: No properties matched the cohort for this chart.

ratesBySeason.subtitle: Avg. Retail Daily Rate Before Taxes and Booking Fees
ratesBySeason.intro: Unweighted mean of each seasonal rate column across classified Campspot and RoverPass rows in the five U.S. regions. A row contributes to a bar only when that column parses within the standard retail rate band ($10–$3,000).
ratesBySeason.methodology: Fields: winter_weekday, winter_weekend, spring_weekday, spring_weekend, summer_weekday, summer_weekend, fall_weekday, fall_weekend. Each value must fall in the standard ARDR band. No taxes or booking-fee columns are added.

siteSurfaceRates.subtitle: Avg. Retail Daily Rates Are Before Taxes and Booking Fees
siteSurfaceRates.intro: Mean 2025 ARDR (avg. retail daily rate) by RV site surface type for classified rows in the selected unit-type slice (same toggle as the rest of the page). Rows must be in the five U.S. regions, have a surface type that maps to Concrete Pad, Loose Gravel, or Grass or Field, and average retail daily rate 2025 inside the standard ARDR band. A dash means no qualifying rows for that bucket (often `rv_surface_type` is blank on non-RV rows, e.g. Lodging).
siteSurfaceRates.methodology: Surface groups use the `rv_surface_type` text field with keyword rules (e.g. paved/concrete/asphalt → Concrete Pad; gravel/dirt/crushed/road base/decomposed granite/caliche → Loose Gravel; grass/turf/field → Grass or Field). Unmapped, blank, or mixed values are excluded.

amenitiesByPropertyPct.subtitle: Share of distinct campgrounds (property name, state, city) with each amenity on at least one row
amenitiesByPropertyPct.intro: Each percentage is the count of distinct properties with that amenity present on any qualifying Campspot or RoverPass row, divided by all distinct properties in the cohort. A row qualifies only when 2025 occupancy and 2025 ARDR pass standard bands; a property is included if it has a property name and U.S. state in one of the five RV industry regions.
amenitiesByPropertyPct.methodology: Present means yes/y/true/1 (case-insensitive) on the column, except 50 amp: also counted when `electrical_hook_up` text contains “50 amp”. Columns: hot_tub_sauna, pool, electrical_hook_up (50 amp only), sewer_hook_up, water_hookup.

amenitiesByAvgAdr.chartTitle: Amenities by Avg. Retail Daily Rate
amenitiesByAvgAdr.subtitle: RV sites were only used in determining these differences, tent sites and lodging units are excluded
amenitiesByAvgAdr.intro: For each amenity, mean 2025 ARDR (avg. retail daily rate) is computed separately for RV site rows with the amenity present (yes/y/true/1) versus without. Only rows classified as RV sites and in the five U.S. regions are included. ARDR uses the average retail daily rate 2025 column only.
amenitiesByAvgAdr.methodology: RV classification uses the same unit-type rules as the Unit Type charts (unit_type, description, property_name). Rows with missing ADR or ADR outside the standard band are excluded from both splits.

stateAdrChoropleth.chartTitle: Avg. Retail Daily Rate by State
stateAdrChoropleth.subtitle: Before Taxes and Booking Fees
stateAdrChoropleth.intro: Mean 2025 ARDR (avg. retail daily rate) by U.S. state for classified Campspot and RoverPass rows in the five RV industry regions. Each row contributes at most once per state when average retail daily rate 2025 parses within the regional ARDR band (RV/Tent $10–$3k; Lodging $10–$10k on this map). Occupancy is not required for this map.
stateAdrChoropleth.methodology: Color scale spans roughly {low}–{high} per night for display; values below or above are shown as the lightest or darkest color. States with no qualifying rows are labeled N/A. States with at least one row but fewer than {minN} qualifying rows are labeled insufficient data and shown in neutral gray. Alaska and Hawaii follow the same rules; Hawaii is drawn in a small inset to the left of Alaska in the lower-left corner.

sageAi.subtitle: Ask questions about glamping properties, reports, and data. Export results as CSV.
sageAi.emptyStateTitle: How can I help you today?
sageAi.emptyStateHint: For feasibility and market work, start by naming a state or region—or use a preset below. I’ll ask for your geography before pulling Sage data. You don’t need database column names: use Field guide (top) or ask in plain English (e.g. “properties with private bathrooms in Colorado”).
sageAi.marketReportBootstrapPreamble: Here is the Sage market report you just ran (numbers below). Use only what is in the report — do not guess. Answer in plain English and keep it short unless I ask for more detail.

sageAi.quickStartRatePrompt: I’m framing market size for an outdoor hospitality feasibility study. Before you answer, ask which U.S. state, region, or corridor I should focus on (one state, several, or a defined area). Then use Sage data to summarize supply: property counts by segment where the data supports it (e.g. glamping vs RV), geographic spread, and what that implies for addressable market scope. Call out data gaps.
sageAi.quickStartRvPrompt: I need a competitive landscape slide for a market analysis / feasibility memo. Before you answer, ask which state or region defines my market. Then use Sage **Campspot and RoverPass** data (not glamping inventory alone) to describe RV comparables, clustering by rate band or product type where visible, and whether supply looks fragmented or concentrated.
sageAi.quickStartUnitPrompt: I’m testing product and programming options for a feasibility study. Before you analyze, ask which U.S. state or region I care about. Then use Sage data on unit types, formats, and mixes that show up most in that market, and briefly note implications for a new entrant’s program.
sageAi.quickStartParksPrompt: I’m assessing demand context—recreation pull and destination draw—for a potential site. Before you answer, ask which state, region, or travel corridor I should focus on. Then use Sage data to relate outdoor-hospitality supply to major draws (e.g. parks, destinations) where the fields support it, and flag where inference is limited.
sageAi.quickStartTexasReportsPrompt: I’m doing due diligence for a feasibility study and want narrative context, not just raw counts. Before you summarize, ask which U.S. state or region I want to focus on. Then combine themes from relevant Sage past reports for that market with what the database shows, and separate opinion from fact.
sageAi.quickStartUsaTrendsPrompt: I want market trends that matter for feasibility—direction of supply, rates, and positioning. Before you dive in, ask whether I want a single state, a multi-state region, or a national lens. Then synthesize trends from Sage data and past reports where useful for that scope, and note uncertainty.
